item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an F-Ray! Kind of. +1

F-Ray
Intriguing. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like this one. I like how it does something very strange, I like how well-thought out the experiments are, I like that the effects on living organisms are equally clearly imagined. I imagine that this one was created for a very particular reason and functions exactly as intended, but the Foundation just hasn't quite figured out yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum 1 essentially ruined it for me, but then Addendum 2 saved it. I think they'd be better off merged as one, or else leaving out Addendum 1 altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I can't but upvote seeing as how this was my idea to begin with - and with me being a couple of months AWOL by the time dragonnyxx published this, I can only be glad someone liked my idea enough to flesh it out, upgrade it and make a good SCP out of it. Anyhow, +1, mate, better job than I would have done - the silo part is a pure win in my book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well reasoned out article. Takes a simple idea and expands on it nicely. My one question is what happens if I try to put stuff into the displacement beam. I assume I can't because material in the silo blocks me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but I find the execution messy. The agent's note seems to me to be a cop-out in order to be able to expand on this thing's effect without having to hint at it or write it up in more clinical wording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The right amount of weird and weird+science without being nonsensical or laborious to read. Echoing TL333s on the unclear bit with the beam, as that is how I imagined it reading through the first time, too. Other than that, this is pretty neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting exploration of the ramifications displacing continues matter has on the edge of the displaced region, plus a weird and inexplicable anomalous object without the whole "why? 'cause SCP!" feel you get from forced weirdness. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. It's actually rather refreshing to have "thing what transports something to somewhere else" end up having a definitive, mundane location for once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. +1 for this, and for molecular splitting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone at work just looked at me like I was a maniac when I laughed. Thanks, Shadow. Thanks a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll have to clarify that — the hole was at an angle, and she was standing on the unaffected ground above it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting concept made better with the report from the silo. I have one quibble, though.

Researcher ████████ walks on the ground above the displaced hole with no ill effect.

I'm trying and failing to figure out how you could walk on the hole without stepping into the beam. I would that would merit a note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nonsense. If the cone damaged no molecules, it would have no effect. If it destroyed ALL molecular bonds along its edge, it would quite literally cut you in half. There are plenty of in-between levels of damage, ranging from a slight increase in cancer risk years from now to killing you in minutes or hours.

In the real world, sufficiently intense radiation exposure has indeed killed people in a matter of hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Careful study has revealed that displacement via SCP-1188 causes microscopic damage to material along the edge of the displaced region. … For living organisms, damaging even a small percentage of the DNA, enzymes, cell walls, and so forth can cause catastrophic damage.

Causing molecular-level damage to cells along the edge of the cone wouldn't affect a enough cells to cause radiation poisoning. It could turn one or more of the cells cancerous, but that would take years to show up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was totally expecting the place the matter was transported to to either be filled with static, or a featureless cube, or completely dark, or some sort of cop-out. Having it be an actual place, that The Foundation could actually reach, makes this a lot richer of an item and takes it from a neutral vote to a +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you trying to [DATA REDACTED] all of us? Don't ask those sorts of questions!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you shone the torch at the silo, would there be some sort of infinite regression as bits of silo are displaced into the silo and the torch beam again, which displaces them into a different bit of the torch beam again and again forever? And is there a way of aligning the torch so it displaces things to exactly where they started from, by positioning the torch where the tip of the Cone of Displacement ends up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A test on the displaced matter would be nice. Can the matter in the silo be affected? If a piece is removed, does it affect the rest when it reintergrates (besides a small hole)?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but it is different enough in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hmm… sure, why not +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my favorite part of this is that the stuff displaced appears somewhere else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You read WHY they die, right? I am one of the "it doesn't HAVE to be nagative" supporters, but this does it well. Without it being tacked on in the slightest, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot.

Might I suggest renaming it something like "Matter-Displacing Flashlight" on the series list? "Flashlight" seems like an awfully generic title considering what's going on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting and well-thought out expansion on a relatively basic concept. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but I don't really like this idea. At first, it reads like the Sonic Blaster from that one Doctor Who episode. The bit about the grain silo is a nice touch that almost made me upvote, but that idea didn't even go anywhere past "Yeah, it moves stuff to the grain silo temporarily." Which we kinda already knew. (Though you do get points for not making it be spooOOOoooOoOooooky and have the camera record static, and stuff like that).

Oh, and this.

Addendum 1
D-16621 became extremely ill 48 hours after exposure, with severe nausea, vomiting, and anemia, dying two days later due to multiple organ failure. Agent ████████'s symptoms were similar but much more acute, with rapidly worsening neurological problems resulting in death six hours after exposure. SCP-1188 is no longer to be used on living organisms.

Reads like:

Addendum 1
OH YEAH, THEY DIED BY THE WAY, BECAUSE DEAD PEOPLE MAKE THE FUCKING GRASS GROW HERE AT THE FOUNDATION AND IT AIN'T A GOOD ARTICLE UNTIL WE HAVE SOME DEAD PEOPLE!! YEAAAAH!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, no, I understood that it was supposed to be a field report, but the tonal shift is really startling. The whole beginning of the field report is very terse and impersonal:

Grain silo found at indicated location. Area surrounding silo is a farm in extreme disrepair, no inhabitants. When activated, the entire cone of matter displaced by SCP-1188 is visible in the air in the exact center of this silo.

…but then you get the last sentence of each paragraph, and they stick out like sore thumbs.

And, while I understand that these are agents making field reports, they're still expected to do so with a certain level of formality. I'm sure that they oft skimp on this expectation, but this feels excessive, at least in contrast to the rest of what was reported.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for catching the mistakes. The tone shift (and use of Imperial units) was deliberate, as that was an informal field report rather than a formal document. I added an attribution to make that more clear, and am not averse to changing it if it continues to bug people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few things I noticed:

+ show block
– hide block

[…] and the sudden shift in orientation was disorienting.

This sounds repetitive when I read it.

It's one of the weirdest things I've ever seen. [and passim ff.]

Woah. Tone shift. Drop this.

[…] dyring […]

Wut?

Otherwise pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this remind anyone else of the Pinwheel of Doom?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting- in a good way- but the tone… Something seems a little off, but I can't tone for toffee so I'll leave that to the experts.
